active.end: Unclear / original version discontinued, later revival appears absent from mainstream retail

hook: A grape theater-box candy with a fragmented search landscape: part discontinued classic, part revival story, and still missing a strong official page that fully explains what happened.
fate: Alexander the Grape was introduced by Ferrara in 1971, discontinued in its earlier form in the 1980s, and later reintroduced under 1908 Candy. Today it appears hard to find, with sold-out retailer pages and no strong current owner page clearly controlling the query.
summary: Alexander the Grape is a retro grape candy brand with deeper history than many nostalgia writeups explain. Public candy-history sources say Ferrara introduced it in 1971 alongside Johnny Apple Treats and Cherry Clan, and that the original version was discontinued in the 1980s. Later, the brand was reintroduced under 1908 Candy, but current retail visibility looks weak. That makes Alexander the Grape a good example of a brand that is not simply 'gone' or 'active' but stuck in a fragmented middle ground of nostalgia, revival, and hard-to-find modern availability.

cultural_memory: Alexander the Grape belongs to the same old theater-box candy world as Johnny Apple Treats and other Ferrara fruit candies.
It is remembered less as a giant national brand and more as the kind of candy people suddenly remember when they see the box again.
That lower mainstream visibility actually makes the nostalgia more personal and the search results more fragmented.

The brand also carries a naming-history wrinkle because some candy-history pages connect it to later 'heads' style branding, which adds to the confusion around what exactly survived and what disappeared.
overview_md:
**Alexander the Grape** is one of those old candy names that looks simple at first and then gets more complicated the closer you look. The strongest candy-history sources say **Ferrara introduced it in 1971**, and that the original version was later **discontinued in the 1980s**. That alone would make it a straightforward discontinued-candy story.

But that is not the full picture. Later candy-history and retailer pages say the brand was **reintroduced under 1908 Candy**. At the same time, current retailer signals are weak. **Sweeties** shows Alexander the Grape as a **discontinued item** and sold out, while **Candy Favorites** also shows sold-out or discontinued product listings. That means the best current description is not simply 'active' or 'dead.' It is more accurate to say the brand has had a **revival attempt**, but still appears **hard to find in mainstream retail**.

That in-between status is exactly what makes Alexander the Grape interesting from a search perspective. The visible results are fragmented: sold-out specialty candy pages, partial history writeups, and nostalgia references, but no strong official page that clearly answers the user’s likely questions.

So the strongest content angle is not just 'Is it discontinued?' It is a fuller answer: **what happened to Alexander the Grape, when was the original discontinued, what came back under 1908 Candy, and can you still buy it today?** That is more useful than the current SERP, which still leaves those questions only partially answered.

timeline.text: Candy Favorites says Ferrara introduced Alexander the Grape alongside Johnny Apple Treats and Cherry Clan.
Candy Favorites says the original Alexander the Grape product was discontinued in the 1980s.
Candy history pages connect Alexander the Grape to later 'heads' branding and flavor-family evolution, which adds some confusion to the brand story.
Candy Favorites says Alexander the Grape was reintroduced by 1908 Candy.
Retailer pages show Alexander the Grape sold out or marked as discontinued, suggesting the revival does not currently have strong mainstream availability.

faq.question: Is Alexander the Grape discontinued?
faq.answer: The original Ferrara-era version was discontinued in the 1980s. The brand was later reintroduced under 1908 Candy, but current retailer pages suggest it is still hard to find and may again be absent from mainstream retail.

faq.question: When did Alexander the Grape come out?
faq.answer: Candy Favorites says Ferrara introduced Alexander the Grape in 1971.

faq.question: Did Alexander the Grape come back?
faq.answer: Yes. Candy Favorites says the brand was reintroduced by 1908 Candy in 2020.

faq.question: Why is Alexander the Grape a good fragmented-SERP topic?
faq.answer: Because the current results are scattered across sold-out retailer pages, candy-history references, and nostalgia posts, with no strong official explainer controlling the query.

faq.question: What is the best content angle for Alexander the Grape?
faq.answer: The strongest angle is: what happened to Alexander the Grape, when was the original discontinued, what exactly came back later, and whether you can still buy it today.
